content:
# Exodus Chapter 29 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~50r-51v (PDF pp. 98-100); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 71-73 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_029_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_029_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_029_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_029_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_029_vaticanus_page_003.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- THE CONSECRATION OF THE PRIESTS:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 29 across two folio pages in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This chapter completes the transition begun in chapter 28 -- from garments to ceremony, from priestly clothing to priestly consecration. The chapter moves through a carefully ordered ritual sequence: washing and vesting (vv. 1-9), the bull sin offering (vv. 10-14), the first ram as burnt offering (vv. 15-18), the ram of ordination with its unique blood ritual (vv. 19-28), the succession of garments (vv. 29-30), the sacred ordination meal (vv. 31-34), the seven-day repetition (vv. 35-37), the daily offerings (vv. 38-42), and YHWH's promise to dwell among His people (vv. 43-46). The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 71-73) presents the LXX text across three pages, following the MT sequence closely with minor expansions.

---

## Washing and Vesting of the Priests (vv. 1-9)

**29:1** "And this is the thing which you shall do to them to consecrate them to serve as priest to me: take one young bull, a son of the herd, and two rams without blemish, [fn1]

**29:2** and bread of unleavened bread and cakes of unleavened bread mixed with oil, and wafers of unleavened bread anointed with oil; of fine wheat flour you shall make them. [fn2]

**29:3** And you shall put them into one basket, and you shall bring them near in the basket, and the bull and the two rams. [fn3]

**29:4** And Aaron and his sons you shall bring near to the entrance of the tent of meeting, and you shall wash them with water. [fn4]

**29:5** And you shall take the garments, and you shall clothe Aaron with the tunic and the robe of the ephod and the ephod and the breastpiece; and you shall gird him with the skillfully woven band of the ephod. [fn5]

**29:6** And you shall set the turban upon his head, and you shall put the crown of holiness upon the turban. [fn6]

**29:7** And you shall take the oil of anointing, and you shall pour it upon his head, and you shall anoint him. [fn7]

**29:8** And his sons you shall bring near, and you shall clothe them with tunics. [fn8]

**29:9** And you shall gird them with sashes -- Aaron and his sons -- and you shall bind headdresses on them; and the priesthood shall be theirs as a statute forever; and you shall fill the hand of Aaron and the hand of his sons. [fn9]

---

## The Bull of the Sin Offering (vv. 10-14)

**29:10** And you shall bring near the bull before the tent of meeting, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands upon the head of the bull. [fn10]

**29:11** And you shall slaughter the bull before YHWH, at the entrance of the tent of meeting. [fn11]

**29:12** And you shall take from the blood of the bull, and you shall put it upon the horns of the altar with your finger; and all the remaining blood you shall pour out at the base of the altar. [fn12]

**29:13** And you shall take all the fat covering the innards, and the appendage upon the liver, and the two kidneys and the fat which is upon them, and you shall turn them to smoke on the altar. [fn13]

**29:14** And the flesh of the bull and its skin and its dung you shall burn with fire outside the camp; it is a sin offering. [fn14]

---

## The First Ram -- Burnt Offering (vv. 15-18)

**29:15** And the one ram you shall take, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands upon the head of the ram. [fn15]

**29:16** And you shall slaughter the ram, and you shall take its blood, and you shall dash it against the altar all around. [fn16]

**29:17** And the ram you shall cut into its pieces, and you shall wash its innards and its legs, and you shall put them upon its pieces and upon its head. [fn17]

**29:18** And you shall turn to smoke the whole ram on the altar; it is a burnt offering to YHWH, a pleasing aroma, an offering by fire to YHWH. [fn18]

---

## The Ram of Ordination (vv. 19-28)

**29:19** And you shall take the second ram, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands upon the head of the ram. [fn19]

**29:20** And you shall slaughter the ram, and you shall take from its blood, and you shall put it upon the lobe of the right ear of Aaron and upon the lobe of the right ear of his sons, and upon the thumb of their right hand and upon the great toe of their right foot; and you shall dash the blood against the altar all around. [fn20]

**29:21** And you shall take from the blood which is upon the altar and from the oil of anointing, and you shall sprinkle it upon Aaron and upon his garments, and upon his sons and upon the garments of his sons with him; and he shall be consecrated -- he and his garments and his sons and the garments of his sons with him. [fn21]

**29:22** And you shall take from the ram the fat and the fat tail and the fat covering the innards, and the appendage of the liver, and the two kidneys and the fat which is upon them, and the right thigh -- for it is a ram of ordination -- [fn22]

**29:23** and one round loaf of bread and one cake of oiled bread and one wafer from the basket of unleavened bread which is before YHWH. [fn23]

**29:24** And you shall place all upon the palms of Aaron and upon the palms of his sons, and you shall wave them as a wave offering before YHWH. [fn24]

**29:25** And you shall take them from their hands, and you shall turn them to smoke on the altar upon the burnt offering, as a pleasing aroma before YHWH; it is an offering by fire to YHWH. [fn25]

**29:26** And you shall take the breast from the ram of ordination which is for Aaron, and you shall wave it as a wave offering before YHWH; and it shall be your portion. [fn26]

**29:27** And you shall consecrate the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution which was waved and which was raised, from the ram of ordination -- from that which is for Aaron and from that which is for his sons. [fn27]

**29:28** And it shall be for Aaron and for his sons as a statute forever from the Sons of Israel; for it is a contribution, and a contribution it shall be from the Sons of Israel from their peace offerings -- their contribution to YHWH. [fn28]

---

## The Succession of Garments (vv. 29-30)

**29:29** And the garments of holiness which are for Aaron shall be for his sons after him, to be anointed in them and to fill their hand in them. [fn29]

**29:30** Seven days the priest from among his sons who succeeds him shall wear them, he who comes to the tent of meeting to minister in the holy place. [fn30]

---

## The Ordination Meal (vv. 31-34)

**29:31** And the ram of ordination you shall take, and you shall boil its flesh in a holy place. [fn31]

**29:32** And Aaron and his sons shall eat the flesh of the ram and the bread which is in the basket, at the entrance of the tent of meeting. [fn32]

**29:33** And they shall eat them -- those things by which atonement was made to fill their hand, to consecrate them; and a stranger shall not eat, for they are holy. [fn33]

**29:34** And if any of the flesh of the ordination or of the bread remains until the morning, then you shall burn the remainder with fire; it shall not be eaten, for it is holy. [fn34]

---

## Seven Days of Consecration (vv. 35-37)

**29:35** And you shall do thus to Aaron and to his sons, according to all that I have commanded you; seven days you shall fill their hand. [fn35]

**29:36** And a bull of sin offering you shall offer each day for the atonements; and you shall purify the altar by making atonement upon it, and you shall anoint it to consecrate it. [fn36]

**29:37** Seven days you shall make atonement upon the altar and you shall consecrate it; and the altar shall be most holy; all that touches the altar shall be holy. [fn37]

---

## The Daily Offerings -- Tamid (vv. 38-42)

**29:38** And this is what you shall offer upon the altar: lambs, sons of a year, two each day, continually. [fn38]

**29:39** The one lamb you shall offer in the morning, and the second lamb you shall offer between the two evenings. [fn39]

**29:40** And a tenth measure of fine flour mixed with beaten oil, a quarter of a hin; and a drink offering, a quarter of a hin of wine, for the one lamb. [fn40]

**29:41** And the second lamb you shall offer between the two evenings; like the grain offering of the morning and like its drink offering you shall offer for it, as a pleasing aroma, an offering by fire to YHWH. [fn41]

**29:42** A continual burnt offering throughout your generations, at the entrance of the tent of meeting before YHWH, where I will meet with you there, to speak to you there. [fn42]

---

## The Divine Promise of Dwelling (vv. 43-46)

**29:43** And I will meet there with the Sons of Israel, and it shall be consecrated by my glory. [fn43]

**29:44** And I will consecrate the tent of meeting and the altar; and Aaron and his sons I will consecrate to serve as priest to me. [fn44]

**29:45** And I will dwell in the midst of the Sons of Israel, and I will be their God. [fn45]

**29:46** And they shall know that I am YHWH their God, who brought them out from the land of Egypt to dwell in their midst; I am YHWH their God. [fn46]

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Note

Exodus 29 is the ritual complement to the sartorial instructions of chapter 28. If chapter 28 answered "What shall the priests wear?", chapter 29 answers "How shall the priests be made?" The chapter moves through a carefully ordered sequence: preparation (vv. 1-3), washing and vesting (vv. 4-9), the three sacrifices of ordination (vv. 10-28), provisions for succession and the sacred meal (vv. 29-34), the seven-day framework (vv. 35-37), the perpetual daily offering (vv. 38-42), and the climactic divine promise (vv. 43-46). The three sacrifices form the structural and theological center: the bull as *chattat* (sin offering, vv. 10-14) purifies; the first ram as *olah* (burnt offering, vv. 15-18) consecrates wholly; and the second ram as *eil millu'im* (ram of ordination, vv. 19-28) effects the actual ordination through blood on the extremities and the filling of the priests' hands. This threefold sequence -- purification, consecration, ordination -- provides the template for Leviticus 8, where the ceremony is performed. The chapter climaxes not in ritual but in relationship: YHWH's declaration "I will dwell in the midst of the Sons of Israel" (v. 45) is the telos of the entire tabernacle project (chapters 25-29).

### Key Decisions

**"To serve as priest" for *lekhahano li* (vv. 1, 44):** Consistent with the decision at 28:1. EMET renders "to be a priest to me." LOGOS renders "to minister as priest to Me." PNEUMA renders "to serve me as priests." The consensus adopts "to serve as priest to me" -- capturing the piel denominative force of *kahan* and the relational direction of *li* ("to me").

**"Sin offering" for *chattat* (vv. 14, 36):** EMET renders "sin offering." LOGOS renders "sin offering." PNEUMA renders "purification offering." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "sin offering" is the traditional and more widely recognized rendering. PNEUMA's "purification offering" captures the piel function of the *chattat* ritual (purging impurity) but obscures the etymological connection to *chet* ("sin, missing the mark"). Both renderings have scholarly support; the consensus preserves the traditional term while noting the purificatory function in the footnotes.

**"Turn to smoke" for *hiqtir* (vv. 13, 18, 25):** EMET renders "turn to smoke." LOGOS renders "turn into smoke." PNEUMA renders "burn." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "turn to smoke" for *hiqtir* (hiphil of *qatar*) preserves the distinction between *qatar* ("to cause to become fragrant smoke" -- the sacrificial burning on the altar) and *saraf* ("to burn" -- the destructive burning outside the camp, v. 14). PNEUMA's "burn" collapses this critical distinction.

**"Dash" for *zaraq* (vv. 16, 20):** EMET renders "throw." LOGOS renders "dash." PNEUMA renders "splash." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "dash" for *zaraq* conveys the forceful, directed action of casting blood against the altar, distinguishing it from the finger-applied blood of v. 12. EMET's "throw" is accurate but less specific; PNEUMA's "splash" suggests an incidental result rather than a deliberate act.

**"Appendage" for *yoteret* (vv. 13, 22):** EMET renders "appendage." LOGOS renders "lobe." PNEUMA renders "lobe." The consensus follows EMET -- "appendage" for *yoteret* (from *yatar*, "to remain, exceed") preserves the vagueness of the Hebrew term, whose precise anatomical referent is debated (possibly the caudate lobe of the liver, or an appendage of fat upon it). LOGOS/PNEUMA's "lobe" is more specific than the Hebrew warrants.

**"Anointed" for *meshuchim* (v. 2):** EMET renders "anointed." LOGOS renders "smeared." PNEUMA renders "spread." The consensus follows EMET -- "anointed" for *meshuchim* (qal passive participle of *mashach*) preserves the sacral connotation of the verb, which is the same root used for anointing priests and kings. LOGOS's "smeared" is technically accurate but lacks the ritual register; PNEUMA's "spread" is too generic.

**"Fill the hand" for *mille yad* (vv. 9, 24, 29, 33, 35):** Consistent with the decision at 28:41. EMET renders "fill the hand." LOGOS renders "ordain." PNEUMA renders "ordain." The consensus follows EMET -- "fill the hand" preserves the concrete Hebrew idiom for priestly ordination, which derives from placing sacrificial portions into the priest's hands (v. 24). The metaphor communicates that priestly authority is received, not self-generated.

**"Headdresses" for *migba'ot* (v. 9):** Consistent with the decision at 28:40. EMET renders "headdresses." LOGOS renders "caps." PNEUMA renders "headbands." The consensus follows EMET -- "headdresses" preserves the formal dignity of the term and distinguishes these from Aaron's *mitsnefet* ("turban").

**"Statute forever" for *chuqqat olam* / *choq olam* (vv. 9, 28):** Consistent with the decisions at 27:21 and 28:43. EMET renders "statute of perpetuity." LOGOS renders "perpetual statute" / "statute forever." PNEUMA renders "lasting ordinance" / "permanent share." The consensus adopts "statute forever" -- the most natural English rendering of the construct chain.

**"Between the two evenings" for *bein ha'arbayim* (vv. 39, 41):** EMET renders "between the evenings." LOGOS renders "between the two evenings." PNEUMA renders "at twilight." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "between the two evenings" preserves the dual form of *erev* ("evening"), which is distinctive and theologically significant as a liminal time-marker. PNEUMA's "at twilight" is interpretive, collapsing the Hebrew's deliberate ambiguity into a single English term.

**"Pleasing aroma" for *reiach nichoach* (vv. 18, 25, 41):** All three translators agree: "pleasing aroma." The consensus follows unanimously. *Reiach* ("aroma, scent") + *nichoach* ("soothing, restful," from *nuach*, "to rest") -- the ascending smoke brings YHWH satisfaction.

**"Offering by fire" for *ishsheh* (vv. 18, 25, 41):** EMET renders "offering by fire." LOGOS renders "offering by fire." PNEUMA renders "fire offering." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "offering by fire" for *ishsheh* (from *esh*, "fire") is more precise than PNEUMA's compressed form, though both are acceptable.

**"Innards" for *qerev* (vv. 13, 17, 22):** EMET renders "innards." LOGOS renders "entrails." PNEUMA renders "internal organs." The consensus follows EMET -- "innards" for *qerev* (from *qarav*, "to be near, to approach" -- i.e., what is "inside, near the center") is plain, accurate, and avoids the clinical tone of PNEUMA's "internal organs" while being more current than LOGOS's "entrails."

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 29 (pages 71-73) presents the LXX rendering with close correspondence to the MT in structure and content. Notable features: (1) At v. 1, the LXX renders *leqaddesh otam* as *hagiasai autous* ("to consecrate them"). (2) At v. 9, the ordination phrase *mille'ta yad* is rendered *teleiōseis tas cheiras* ("you shall perfect the hands"), maintaining the LXX's consistent use of *teleioō* for *millu'im* -- the noun from which the Epistle to the Hebrews draws its vocabulary of priestly "perfection" (Heb 7:11). (3) At v. 14, the sin offering is rendered *peri hamartias estin* ("it is concerning sin"), the same phrase Paul echoes in Romans 8:3. (4) At v. 18, the burnt offering is described as *holokautōma kuriō* ("whole burnt offering to the Lord") with *osmē euōdias* ("aroma of fragrance"), language Paul applies to Christ's self-offering in Ephesians 5:2. (5) At v. 20, the application of blood to the ear lobe, thumb, and great toe follows the MT closely. (6) At vv. 22-25, the ordination ram instructions preserve the distinctive term *teleiōsis* for the ordination rite. (7) At v. 35, the seven-day period is rendered *hepta hēmeras teleiōseis tas cheiras autōn* ("seven days you shall perfect their hands"). (8) At vv. 38-42, the daily *tamid* offering is rendered with *dia pantos* ("continually") for *tamid*, and *bein ha'arbayim* as *to pros hesperas* ("toward evening"). (9) At v. 45, the climactic declaration reads *kai kataskēnōsō en meso huiōn Israēl kai esomai autōn theos* ("and I will tabernacle in the midst of the sons of Israel and I will be their God"), using *kataskēnoō*, the verb cognate with *skēnē* ("tent, tabernacle"), which John 1:14 echoes in its declaration that the Word "tabernacled" (*eskēnōsen*) among us. (10) At v. 46, the LXX renders the exodus purpose clause as *tou kataskēnōsai en autois* ("to tabernacle among them"), making explicit the tabernacle theology.

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Ve-zeh ha-davar asher-ta'aseh lahem le-qaddesh otam le-khahen li leqach par ekhad ben-baqar ve-eilim shnayim temimim*: "And this is the thing which you shall do to them to consecrate them to serve as priest to me: take one young bull, a son of the herd, and two rams without blemish"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Le-qaddesh* (piel infinitive construct of *qadash*, "to be holy") -- "to consecrate, to make holy"; the entire chapter answers the question: how does a mortal become fit to stand before YHWH? *Temimim* ("without blemish") -- from *tamam* ("to be complete, whole"); the animals must be physically unblemished as a condition for sacred use. EMET renders "to be priests to me"; LOGOS renders "to minister as priests to Me"; PNEUMA renders "to serve me as priests." The consensus adopts "to serve as priest to me" -- consistent with 28:1 (see Key Decisions).

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Ve-lekhem matstsot ve-khallot matstsot belulot ba-shemen u-reqiqei matstsot meshuchim ba-shamen solet khittim ta'aseh otam*: "And bread of unleavened bread and cakes of unleavened bread mixed with oil, and wafers of unleavened bread anointed with oil; of fine wheat flour you shall make them"** -- Three types of bread: plain unleavened bread (*lekhem matstsot*), oil-mixed cakes (*khallot matstsot belulot ba-shemen*), and oil-anointed wafers (*reqiqei matstsot meshuchim ba-shamen*). EMET renders *meshuchim* as "anointed"; LOGOS renders "smeared"; PNEUMA renders "spread." The consensus follows EMET -- "anointed" preserves the sacral register of *mashach* (see Key Decisions). *Solet khittim* ("fine wheat flour") -- the highest quality flour.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Ve-natattah otam al-sal ekhad ve-hiqravta otam ba-sal ve-et-ha-par ve-et shnei ha-eilim*: "And you shall put them into one basket, and you shall bring them near in the basket, and the bull and the two rams"** -- All three translators agree. *Sal* ("basket") -- the bread offerings are presented together in a single basket alongside the animals.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Ve-et-Aharon ve-et-banav taqriv el-petakh ohel mo'ed ve-rakhatsta otam ba-mayim*: "And Aaron and his sons you shall bring near to the entrance of the tent of meeting, and you shall wash them with water"** -- All three translators agree. *Taqriv* (hiphil imperfect of *qarav*) -- "you shall cause to draw near"; the same root that produces *qorban* ("offering"). The priests are brought near like offerings. *Ve-rakhatsta* (qal perfect of *rachats*) -- "you shall wash"; water purification is the first act of consecration.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Ve-laqakhta et-ha-begadim ve-hilbashta et-Aharon et-ha-khetonet ve-et me'il ha-efod ve-et-ha-efod ve-et-ha-khoshen ve-afadeta lo be-kheshev ha-efod*: "And you shall take the garments, and you shall clothe Aaron with the tunic and the robe of the ephod and the ephod and the breastpiece; and you shall gird him with the skillfully woven band of the ephod"** -- All three translators agree. *Ve-afadeta* (qal perfect of *afad*, "to gird, bind on") -- a verb used only for fastening the ephod; denominative from *efod*. The vesting sequence proceeds from innermost garment (tunic) outward.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Ve-samta ha-mitsnefet al-rosho ve-natattah et-nezer ha-qodesh al-ha-mitsnefet*: "And you shall set the turban upon his head, and you shall put the crown of holiness upon the turban"** -- All three translators agree. *Nezer* ("crown, consecration, diadem") -- from *nazar* ("to separate, consecrate"); the gold plate (*tsits*) of 28:36 is here called *nezer* -- it is both a crown and a mark of separation. PNEUMA renders "sacred crown"; the consensus preserves the construct chain "crown of holiness."

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Ve-laqakhta et-shemen ha-mishkhah ve-yatsaqta al-rosho u-mashakhta oto*: "And you shall take the oil of anointing, and you shall pour it upon his head, and you shall anoint him"** -- All three translators agree. *Yatsaq* ("to pour") suggests abundance -- not a dab but a drenching (cf. Ps 133:2). *Shemen ha-mishkhah* ("oil of anointing") -- the special compound described in 30:22-33.

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Ve-et-banav taqriv ve-hilbashtam kuttonot*: "And his sons you shall bring near, and you shall clothe them with tunics"** -- All three translators agree. The ordinary priests receive tunics but not the full array of the high priest's garments.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Ve-khagarta otam avnet Aharon u-vanav ve-khavashta lahem migba'ot ve-hayetah lahem kehunnah le-khuqqat olam u-milleita yad-Aharon ve-yad-banav*: "And you shall gird them with sashes -- Aaron and his sons -- and you shall bind headdresses on them; and the priesthood shall be theirs as a statute forever; and you shall fill the hand of Aaron and the hand of his sons"** -- EMET renders *migba'ot* as "headdresses"; LOGOS renders "caps"; PNEUMA renders "headbands." The consensus follows EMET -- consistent with 28:40 (see Key Decisions). *U-milleita yad* ("and you shall fill the hand") -- the idiom for ordination; literally placing the offering portions into the priest's hands. The consensus preserves the concrete idiom (see Key Decisions).

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Ve-hiqravta et-ha-par lifnei ohel mo'ed ve-samakh Aharon u-vanav et-yedeihem al-rosh ha-par*: "And you shall bring near the bull before the tent of meeting, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands upon the head of the bull"** -- All three translators agree. *Ve-samakh* (qal perfect of *samakh*, "to lean, lay, press") -- the hand-laying transfers identity; the offerer identifies with the animal.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Ve-shakhatta et-ha-par lifnei YHWH petakh ohel mo'ed*: "And you shall slaughter the bull before YHWH, at the entrance of the tent of meeting"** -- All three translators agree. The slaughter takes place at the entrance, in YHWH's presence.

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Ve-laqakhta mi-dam ha-par ve-natattah al-qarnot ha-mizbeakh be-etsba'ekha ve-et-kol-ha-dam tishpokh el-yesod ha-mizbeakh*: "And you shall take from the blood of the bull, and you shall put it upon the horns of the altar with your finger; and all the remaining blood you shall pour out at the base of the altar"** -- All three translators agree. *Be-etsba'ekha* ("with your finger") -- the blood application to the altar horns is manual, not by dashing; an intimate, deliberate act, contrasting with the *zaraq* ("dash") of the burnt offering blood.

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Ve-laqakhta et-kol-ha-khelev ha-mekhasseh et-ha-qerev ve-et ha-yoteret al-ha-kaved ve-et shtei ha-kelayot ve-et-ha-khelev asher aleihen ve-hiqtarta ha-mizbkhah*: "And you shall take all the fat covering the innards, and the appendage upon the liver, and the two kidneys and the fat which is upon them, and you shall turn them to smoke on the altar"** -- EMET renders *yoteret* as "appendage"; LOGOS renders "lobe"; PNEUMA renders "lobe." The consensus follows EMET (see Key Decisions). *Ve-hiqtarta* (hiphil perfect of *qatar*) -- "turn to smoke," not "burn"; the fat ascends as fragrant smoke to YHWH. See Key Decisions on *hiqtir* vs. *saraf*.

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *U-vesar ha-par ve-oro u-firsho tisrof ba-esh mi-khutz la-makhaneh chattat hu*: "And the flesh of the bull and its skin and its dung you shall burn with fire outside the camp; it is a sin offering"** -- EMET renders "sin offering." LOGOS renders "sin offering." PNEUMA renders "purification offering." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS (see Key Decisions). *Chattat* (from *chata*, "to miss, sin") -- the bull's flesh is not eaten but burned entirely outside the camp, distinguishing the sin offering from other sacrifices. The burning outside the camp (*mi-khutz la-makhaneh*) signals the removal of impurity from the sacred precincts -- a detail to which Hebrews 13:11-13 later attaches typological significance.

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Ve-et-ha-ayil ha-ekhad tiqqakh ve-samkhu Aharon u-vanav et-yedeihem al-rosh ha-ayil*: "And the one ram you shall take, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands upon the head of the ram"** -- All three translators agree. *Ha-ayil* ("the ram") -- from *ul* ("to be strong"); the first of two rams, each serving a distinct sacrificial function.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Ve-shakhatta et-ha-ayil ve-laqakhta et-damo ve-zaraqta al-ha-mizbeakh saviv*: "And you shall slaughter the ram, and you shall take its blood, and you shall dash it against the altar all around"** -- EMET renders *zaraq* as "throw." LOGOS renders "dash." PNEUMA renders "splash." The consensus follows LOGOS (see Key Decisions). The blood is dashed against the altar sides, not dabbed with the finger as in the sin offering.

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Ve-et-ha-ayil tenattakh li-ntakhav ve-rakhatsta et-qirbo ve-et-kera'av ve-natattah al-netakhav ve-al-rosho*: "And the ram you shall cut into its pieces, and you shall wash its innards and its legs, and you shall put them upon its pieces and upon its head"** -- All three translators agree. *Tenattakh* (piel imperfect of *natakh*) -- ritual dismemberment for orderly arrangement on the altar.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Ve-hiqtarta et-kol-ha-ayil ha-mizbkhah olah hu la-YHWH reiach nichoach ishsheh hu la-YHWH*: "And you shall turn to smoke the whole ram on the altar; it is a burnt offering to YHWH, a pleasing aroma, an offering by fire to YHWH"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Olah* ("burnt offering") -- from *alah* ("to go up, ascend"); the entire animal ascends as smoke. *Reiach nichoach* ("a pleasing aroma") -- from *nuach* ("to rest"); the ascending smoke brings YHWH satisfaction. PNEUMA renders *hiqtir* as "burn," which the consensus corrects to "turn to smoke" (see Key Decisions).

> **[fn19] v.19 -- *Ve-laqakhta et ha-ayil ha-sheni ve-samakh Aharon u-vanav et-yedeihem al-rosh ha-ayil*: "And you shall take the second ram, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands upon the head of the ram"** -- All three translators agree. The second ram is the distinctive *eil millu'im* ("ram of ordination," v. 22).

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *Ve-shakhatta et-ha-ayil ve-laqakhta mi-damo ve-natattah al-tenukh ozen Aharon ve-al-tenukh ozen banav ha-yemanit ve-al-bohen yadam ha-yemanit ve-al-bohen raglam ha-yemanit ve-zaraqta et-ha-dam al-ha-mizbeakh saviv*: "And you shall slaughter the ram, and you shall take from its blood, and you shall put it upon the lobe of the right ear of Aaron and upon the lobe of the right ear of his sons, and upon the thumb of their right hand and upon the great toe of their right foot; and you shall dash the blood against the altar all around"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Tenukh* ("lobe, tip") -- the extremities of the body (ear, thumb, toe) are consecrated; the priest hears, acts, and walks in holiness. The same three-point ritual appears in Leviticus 14 for the cleansing of the leper, suggesting a deep connection between ordination and restoration to wholeness.

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Ve-laqakhta min-ha-dam asher al-ha-mizbeakh u-mi-shemen ha-mishkhah ve-hizzita al-Aharon ve-al-begadav ve-al-banav ve-al-bigdei vanav itto ve-qadash hu u-vegadav u-vanav u-vigdei vanav itto*: "And you shall take from the blood which is upon the altar and from the oil of anointing, and you shall sprinkle it upon Aaron and upon his garments, and upon his sons and upon the garments of his sons with him; and he shall be consecrated -- he and his garments and his sons and the garments of his sons with him"** -- All three translators agree. *Ve-hizzita* (hiphil perfect of *nazah*, "to sprinkle") -- blood and oil mingled together sprinkled upon persons and garments; the consecration permeates both the priest and his vestments.

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *Ve-laqakhta min-ha-ayil ha-khelev ve-ha-alyah ve-et-ha-khelev ha-mekhasseh et-ha-qerev ve-et yoteret ha-kaved ve-et shtei ha-kelayot ve-et-ha-khelev asher aleihen ve-et shoq ha-yamin ki eil millu'im hu*: "And you shall take from the ram the fat and the fat tail and the fat covering the innards, and the appendage of the liver, and the two kidneys and the fat which is upon them, and the right thigh -- for it is a ram of ordination"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Eil millu'im* ("ram of ordination/fillings") -- from *male* ("to fill"); the ram whose portions will "fill the hands" of the priests. *Alyah* ("fat tail") -- the distinctive fat tail of the broad-tailed sheep common in the ancient Near East.

> **[fn23] v.23 -- *Ve-kikkar lekhem akhat ve-khallat lekhem shemen akhat ve-raqiq ekhad mi-sal ha-matstsot asher lifnei YHWH*: "And one round loaf of bread and one cake of oiled bread and one wafer from the basket of unleavened bread which is before YHWH"** -- All three translators agree. *Kikkar* ("round loaf") -- from *karar* ("to be round"); one of each bread type from v. 2 is selected.

> **[fn24] v.24 -- *Ve-samta ha-kol al kappei Aharon ve-al kappei vanav ve-henafta otam tenufah lifnei YHWH*: "And you shall place all upon the palms of Aaron and upon the palms of his sons, and you shall wave them as a wave offering before YHWH"** -- All three translators agree. *Tenufah* ("wave offering") -- from *nuf* ("to wave, swing"); the priest's filled hands are waved before YHWH. This is the literal "hand-filling" that constitutes ordination.

> **[fn25] v.25 -- *Ve-laqakhta otam mi-yadam ve-hiqtarta ha-mizbkhah al-ha-olah le-reiach nichoach lifnei YHWH ishsheh hu la-YHWH*: "And you shall take them from their hands, and you shall turn them to smoke on the altar upon the burnt offering, as a pleasing aroma before YHWH; it is an offering by fire to YHWH"** -- All three translators agree in substance. The fat portions and bread are burned on top of the burnt offering already on the altar.

> **[fn26] v.26 -- *Ve-laqakhta et-he-khazeh me-eil ha-millu'im asher le-Aharon ve-henafta oto tenufah lifnei YHWH ve-hayah lekha le-manah*: "And you shall take the breast from the ram of ordination which is for Aaron, and you shall wave it as a wave offering before YHWH; and it shall be your portion"** -- All three translators agree. *Khazeh* ("breast") -- the breast portion goes to Moses as the officiant of this unique ceremony.

> **[fn27] v.27 -- *Ve-qiddashta et khazeh ha-tenufah ve-et shoq ha-terumah asher hunaf va-asher huram me-eil ha-millu'im me-asher le-Aharon u-me-asher le-vanav*: "And you shall consecrate the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution which was waved and which was raised, from the ram of ordination -- from that which is for Aaron and from that which is for his sons"** -- All three translators agree. *Terumah* ("contribution, raised offering") -- from *rum* ("to be high, to raise"); the thigh is "raised up" as YHWH's portion. The distinction between *tenufah* (horizontal waving) and *terumah* (vertical raising) establishes two modes of presenting offerings.

> **[fn28] v.28 -- *Ve-hayah le-Aharon u-le-vanav le-khoq-olam me-et benei Yisrael ki terumah hu u-terumah yihyeh me-et benei Yisrael mi-zivkhei shalmeihem terumatam la-YHWH*: "And it shall be for Aaron and for his sons as a statute forever from the Sons of Israel; for it is a contribution, and a contribution it shall be from the Sons of Israel from their peace offerings -- their contribution to YHWH"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Shelamim* ("peace offerings") -- from *shalam* ("to be complete, whole"); the only sacrifice where the offerer, priest, and YHWH all share in the meal. PNEUMA renders *shelamim* as "fellowship offerings," which captures the communal dimension but replaces the Hebrew root (*shalom*) with an English interpretation. The consensus preserves "peace offerings."

> **[fn29] v.29 -- *U-vigdei ha-qodesh asher le-Aharon yihyu le-vanav akharav le-moshkhah bahem u-le-mallei vam et-yadam*: "And the garments of holiness which are for Aaron shall be for his sons after him, to be anointed in them and to fill their hand in them"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Le-moshkhah* ("for anointing") -- the garments are inherited and used in each successive ordination; the priesthood is dynastic. PNEUMA paraphrases "so that they may be anointed and ordained in them" -- the consensus preserves the concrete idiom "to fill their hand."

> **[fn30] v.30 -- *Shiv'at yamim yilbasham ha-kohen takhtav mi-banav asher yavo el-ohel mo'ed le-sharet ba-qodesh*: "Seven days the priest from among his sons who succeeds him shall wear them, he who comes to the tent of meeting to minister in the holy place"** -- All three translators agree. *Takhtav* ("in his place, after him") -- from *takhat* ("under, instead of"); succession is spatial -- the next priest stands "in the place of" the former. *Shiv'at yamim* ("seven days") -- a full creation-cycle of consecration.

> **[fn31] v.31 -- *Ve-et eil ha-millu'im tiqqakh u-vishshalta et-besaro be-maqom qadosh*: "And the ram of ordination you shall take, and you shall boil its flesh in a holy place"** -- All three translators agree. *U-vishshalta* (piel perfect of *bashal*, "to boil, cook") -- the ordination ram's flesh is cooked, not burned; it will be eaten as a consecration meal.

> **[fn32] v.32 -- *Ve-akhal Aharon u-vanav et-besar ha-ayil ve-et-ha-lekhem asher ba-sal petakh ohel mo'ed*: "And Aaron and his sons shall eat the flesh of the ram and the bread which is in the basket, at the entrance of the tent of meeting"** -- All three translators agree. The priests eat the sacred meal at the entrance of YHWH's dwelling; eating in God's presence is the culmination of ordination.

> **[fn33] v.33 -- *Ve-akhelu otam asher kuppar bahem le-mallei et-yadam le-qaddesh otam ve-zar lo yokhal ki-qodesh hem*: "And they shall eat them -- those things by which atonement was made to fill their hand, to consecrate them; and a stranger shall not eat, for they are holy"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Kuppar* (pual perfect of *kafar*, "to cover, atone") -- "atonement was made." *Ve-zar* ("and a stranger/outsider") -- anyone not of the priestly line; the sacred meal is restricted. PNEUMA paraphrases the central clause as "the things with which atonement was made at their ordination and consecration" -- the consensus preserves the Hebrew syntax: "by which atonement was made to fill their hand, to consecrate them."

> **[fn34] v.34 -- *Ve-im-yivvater mi-besar ha-millu'im u-min-ha-lekhem ad-ha-boqer ve-sarafta et-ha-notar ba-esh lo ye'akhel ki-qodesh hu*: "And if any of the flesh of the ordination or of the bread remains until the morning, then you shall burn the remainder with fire; it shall not be eaten, for it is holy"** -- All three translators agree. *Yivvater* (niphal imperfect of *yatar*, "to remain") -- sacred food cannot be stored; holiness has a temporal boundary. Note that *saraf* ("burn") is used here, not *qatar* ("turn to smoke") -- this is destructive burning, not sacrificial smoke.

> **[fn35] v.35 -- *Ve-asita le-Aharon u-le-vanav kakhah ke-khol asher-tsivviti otakh shiv'at yamim temallei yadam*: "And you shall do thus to Aaron and to his sons, according to all that I have commanded you; seven days you shall fill their hand"** -- All three translators agree. *Shiv'at yamim* ("seven days") -- the full cycle of creation; consecration requires a complete week.

> **[fn36] v.36 -- *U-far chattat ta'aseh la-yom al-ha-kippurim ve-khitteta al-ha-mizbeakh be-khaperakh alav u-mashakhta oto le-qadesho*: "And a bull of sin offering you shall offer each day for the atonements; and you shall purify the altar by making atonement upon it, and you shall anoint it to consecrate it"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Ve-khitteta* (piel perfect of *chata*) -- "you shall de-sin, purify"; the piel of *chata* reverses the qal meaning, turning "to sin" into "to purify from sin."

> **[fn37] v.37 -- *Shiv'at yamim tekapper al-ha-mizbeakh ve-qiddashta oto ve-hayah ha-mizbeakh qodesh qodashim kol-ha-nogea ba-mizbeakh yiqdash*: "Seven days you shall make atonement upon the altar and you shall consecrate it; and the altar shall be most holy; all that touches the altar shall be holy"** -- All three translators agree. *Qodesh qodashim* ("holy of holies, most holy") -- the superlative construction; the altar becomes so saturated with holiness that contact with it transmits holiness.

> **[fn38] v.38 -- *Ve-zeh asher ta'aseh al-ha-mizbeakh kevasim benei-shanah shnayim la-yom tamid*: "And this is what you shall offer upon the altar: lambs, sons of a year, two each day, continually"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Tamid* ("continually, perpetually") -- the tamid offering defines the rhythm of Israel's worship; two lambs per day, every day, without ceasing. PNEUMA renders "regularly, every day," which interprets *tamid* rather than translating it. The consensus preserves "continually."

> **[fn39] v.39 -- *Et-ha-keves ha-ekhad ta'aseh ba-boqer ve-et ha-keves ha-sheni ta'aseh bein ha-arbayim*: "The one lamb you shall offer in the morning, and the second lamb you shall offer between the two evenings"** -- EMET renders "between the evenings." LOGOS renders "between the two evenings." PNEUMA renders "at twilight." The consensus follows LOGOS (see Key Decisions). *Bein ha-arbayim* -- the dual form is distinctive and theologically significant.

> **[fn40] v.40 -- *Ve-issaron solet balul be-shemen katit revi'it ha-hin ve-nesekh revi'it ha-hin yayin la-keves ha-ekhad*: "And a tenth measure of fine flour mixed with beaten oil, a quarter of a hin; and a drink offering, a quarter of a hin of wine, for the one lamb"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Katit* ("beaten, crushed") -- from *katat* ("to crush"); the finest quality oil, produced by crushing rather than pressing. *Hin* -- a liquid measure, approximately 6 liters; a quarter-hin is about 1.5 liters.

> **[fn41] v.41 -- *Ve-et ha-keves ha-sheni ta'aseh bein ha-arbayim ke-minkhat ha-boqer u-khe-niskah ta'aseh-lah le-reiach nichoach ishsheh la-YHWH*: "And the second lamb you shall offer between the two evenings; like the grain offering of the morning and like its drink offering you shall offer for it, as a pleasing aroma, an offering by fire to YHWH"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Minkhah* ("grain offering, tribute") -- the accompanying flour-and-oil offering that complements the animal sacrifice.

> **[fn42] v.42 -- *Olat tamid le-doroteikhem petakh ohel-mo'ed lifnei YHWH asher ivvad lakhem shammah le-dabber eleikha sham*: "A continual burnt offering throughout your generations, at the entrance of the tent of meeting before YHWH, where I will meet with you there, to speak to you there"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Ivvad* (niphal imperfect of *ya'ad*, "to appoint, meet by appointment") -- "I will meet by appointment"; YHWH does not encounter Israel randomly but at a designated place and time. The shift from *lakhem* ("to you," plural) to *eleikha* ("to you," singular) is significant: YHWH meets the people at the tent, but speaks to Moses individually there.

> **[fn43] v.43 -- *Ve-no'adti shammah li-vnei Yisrael ve-niqdash bi-khevodi*: "And I will meet there with the Sons of Israel, and it shall be consecrated by my glory"** -- All three translators agree. *Ve-niqdash* (niphal perfect of *qadash*) -- "it shall be consecrated"; the tabernacle is made holy not by human ritual alone but by the weight of YHWH's *kavod* ("glory") filling it.

> **[fn44] v.44 -- *Ve-qiddashti et-ohel mo'ed ve-et-ha-mizbeakh ve-et-Aharon ve-et-banav aqaddesh le-khahen li*: "And I will consecrate the tent of meeting and the altar; and Aaron and his sons I will consecrate to serve as priest to me"** -- All three translators agree. *Aqaddesh* (piel imperfect 1cs) -- "I will consecrate"; YHWH Himself is the ultimate consecrator. The elaborate human rituals of vv. 1-37 find their efficacy in divine action.

> **[fn45] v.45 -- *Ve-shakhanti be-tokh benei Yisrael ve-hayiti lahem le-Elohim*: "And I will dwell in the midst of the Sons of Israel, and I will be their God"** -- All three translators agree. *Ve-shakhanti* (qal perfect of *shakhan*, "to dwell, tabernacle") -- the root of *mishkan* ("tabernacle"); YHWH's dwelling among Israel is the telos of the entire tabernacle project. The entire construction sequence (chapters 25-29) drives toward this single declaration. The covenant formula *ve-hayiti lahem le-Elohim* ("and I will be their God") echoes forward to Jeremiah 31:33, Ezekiel 37:27, and Revelation 21:3.

> **[fn46] v.46 -- *Ve-yad'u ki ani YHWH Eloheihem asher hotseti otam me-erets Mitsrayim le-shokhni be-tokham ani YHWH Eloheihem*: "And they shall know that I am YHWH their God, who brought them out from the land of Egypt to dwell in their midst; I am YHWH their God"** -- All three translators agree. *Le-shokhni* (qal infinitive construct of *shakhan* + 1cs suffix) -- "to dwell, my dwelling"; the Exodus was not ultimately for Israel's freedom but for YHWH's habitation; the purpose of liberation is divine presence. The chapter closes with the repeated covenant formula *ani YHWH Eloheihem* ("I am YHWH their God"), sealing the ordination instructions with the ultimate assurance.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
